title: Getting Around Rio de Janeiro — Transport & Travel Guide
content:
To get around in Rio de Janeiro, you can use a combination of public transportation options such as buses and metro, as well as taxis and ride-hailing services, or explore the city on foot or by car, depending on your preferences and budget.

[FACTBOX]
Main International Airport(s): Rio de Janeiro/Galeão International Airport (GIG), Santos Dumont Airport (SDU)
Domestic Air Network: extensive
Rail Network: limited
Drives On: right
Road Quality: moderate
Ride-Hailing Apps: Uber, 99Taxis
[/FACTBOX]

[SECTION] Getting There [/SECTION]
Rio de Janeiro is easily accessible by air, with two main international airports: Rio de Janeiro/Galeão International Airport (GIG) and Santos Dumont Airport (SDU). Galeão International Airport is the primary hub for international flights, with direct connections to major cities in the Americas, Europe, and other parts of the world. Santos Dumont Airport, on the other hand, is a smaller airport that handles domestic and some international flights. You can also enter Rio de Janeiro by bus or car from neighboring cities and states, with several overland entry points along the city's borders. The city is well-connected to other parts of Brazil, with many bus companies offering frequent services to and from Rio de Janeiro.

If you're coming from other parts of Brazil or South America, you can also take a bus or drive to Rio de Janeiro. The city is located near the intersection of several major highways, making it easily accessible by car. Additionally, many cruise ships stop in Rio de Janeiro, providing another option for visitors to arrive in the city. Regardless of how you arrive, you'll find that Rio de Janeiro is a vibrant and welcoming city, with a wide range of transportation options available to help you get around.

[SECTION] Domestic Flights [/SECTION]
For domestic travel within Brazil, Rio de Janeiro is a major hub with frequent flights to many other cities. You can fly with airlines such as Gol, Azul, and LATAM, which offer a wide range of routes and schedules. Booking in advance is recommended, especially during peak travel seasons, as prices can be high if you wait until the last minute. Approximate costs for domestic flights vary depending on the route and time of year, but you can expect to pay around $100-$300 for a one-way ticket. It's also a good idea to compare prices across different airlines and book your flights through a reputable online travel agency or the airlines' websites directly.

[SECTION] Trains & Rail [/SECTION]
Rio de Janeiro has a limited rail network, with no high-speed trains or extensive commuter rail services. However, there are some tourist trains and scenic rail lines that offer a unique way to see the city and its surroundings. One example is the **Corcovado Rack Railway**, which takes you to the top of Corcovado Mountain and offers stunning views of the city. Booking is usually not necessary, as you can buy tickets at the train station or online in advance. Keep in mind that the rail network is not a primary mode of transportation in Rio de Janeiro, and you may find it more convenient to use buses or other forms of public transportation to get around.

[SECTION] Buses & Coaches [/SECTION]
For intercity bus travel, Rio de Janeiro has a well-developed network of bus companies that offer frequent services to many other cities in Brazil. Companies such as **Greyhound Brasil** and **Ormeño** operate modern buses with comfortable seating and amenities like air conditioning and onboard restrooms. You can book tickets online or at the bus station, and costs vary depending on the route and class of service. A one-way ticket from Rio de Janeiro to São Paulo, for example, might cost around $20-$50. Buses are a convenient and affordable way to travel, especially for shorter distances or if you're on a budget.

[SECTION] City Transport [/SECTION]
| Mode | Available In | Cost (USD) | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Metro/Subway | Rio de Janeiro | $1.20 | Modern and efficient, with four lines and 41 stations |
| City Bus | Rio de Janeiro | $1.20 | Extensive network with many routes and frequent services |
| Taxi | everywhere | $2.50/km | Available at taxi stands or by hailing on the street |
| Ride-Hailing | Rio de Janeiro | $5-$10 | Uber and 99Taxis are widely available |
| Tuk-tuk/Rickshaw | not available | - | Not a common mode of transportation in Rio de Janeiro |

[SECTION] Driving [/SECTION]
- To drive in Rio de Janeiro, you'll need a valid driver's license from your home country or an International Driving Permit.
- Road conditions in Rio de Janeiro are generally moderate, with some roads in good condition and others with potholes and traffic congestion.
- Fuel costs are relatively high in Brazil, with prices around $1.50 per liter for gasoline.
- Car rental companies such as **Hertz** and **Avis** have offices at the airport and in the city, offering a range of vehicles for rent.
- Be aware of potential hazards such as aggressive drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists, and take necessary precautions to stay safe on the road.
- Parking can be challenging in Rio de Janeiro, especially in popular areas like Copacabana and Ipanema, so be prepared to pay for parking or look for alternative options.

[SECTION] Transport Tips [/SECTION]
- Be aware of your surroundings and keep an eye on your belongings, especially in crowded areas and public transportation.
- Use reputable taxi companies or ride-hailing apps to avoid scams and ensure a safe ride.
- Learn some basic Portuguese phrases to help you navigate and communicate with locals.
- Download transportation apps like **Moovit** or **Waze** to help you get around the city and avoid traffic congestion.
- Consider purchasing a **RioCard**, a prepaid card that can be used to pay for public transportation and other services.
- Be prepared for traffic jams and delays, especially during peak hours or special events.
- Stay informed about local events and demonstrations, which can affect traffic and transportation in the city.
- Use cash or credit cards to pay for transportation and other services, as not all places accept foreign currency or debit cards.
